content.problemStatement:
An array AAA is called weird if it can be sorted in non-decreasing order by applying the given operation any number of times:


Select any index iii (1≤i≤∣A∣)(1 \le i \le |A|)(1≤i≤∣A∣) and set Ai:=−AiA_i := -A_iAi​:=−Ai​.
For example: A=[2,1,3]A = [2, 1, 3]A=[2,1,3] is weird since after applying the operation at i=1i = 1i=1, AAA becomes [−2,1,3][-2, 1, 3][−2,1,3] which is sorted.

JJ has a permutation PPP of length NNN. He wants to find the number of subarrays of PPP which are weird. Can you help him?


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the permutation PPP.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \dots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ denoting the permutation PPP.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of weird subarrays of PPP.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105.
